nativeRange.habitat: Found on lagoon and seaward reefs, typically at depths of 3 to 60 m. Adults occupy coral-rich zones, while juveniles tend to stay hidden at moderate depths.

diet.notes: In the wild, has been documented feeding primarily on sponges and tunicates, along with algae and small invertebrates. In captivity, generally advised to offer high-quality sponge-based frozen angelfish preparations, supplemented with mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, spirulina, nori, and marine algae. Feeding small portions at least two to three times daily is commonly recommended. This species has been reported to struggle with adapting to substitute foods; patience and variety tend to improve long-term feeding success.

imageCandidates.notes: Diego Delso, 6135x3723. Tack-sharp full-body lateral profile of a vibrant lemon-yellow adult showing the diagnostic blue lips and all three dark spots (nape + flanks); best composition of the pool.
Diego Delso, 4039x4039. Sharp in-situ reef shot of a bright yellow individual with clear blue lips and three spots; different angle for variety.
Rickard Zerpe, 2960x2220. Sharp full-frame fish clearly showing the white belly band with black margin, blue lips and three spots; second photographer for variety.

summary: A striking Indo-Pacific angelfish with brilliant yellow coloration and blue lips, generally considered challenging to keep due to its dietary dependence on sponge-based foods. Best suited to experienced reef keepers in larger systems.
careNotes: The Flagfin Angelfish is generally considered one of the more demanding large angelfish in the aquarium hobby, primarily due to its specialized dietary requirements; it has been reported to rely heavily on sponge and tunicate material in the wild, and captive specimens may take time to accept prepared foods. A varied diet with high-quality sponge-containing angelfish preparations is commonly advised. Newly introduced fish tend to be shy and may hide for several weeks; providing ample live rock with caves and hiding spots is generally recommended to reduce stress. This species is typically kept singly, as conspecific aggression has been documented. It tends toward semi-aggressive behavior, particularly toward other large angelfish. This species is not generally considered reef-safe; it has been known to nip at soft corals, stony corals, and clam mantles, though some hobbyists have reported success in large mixed systems with caution. It is a protogynous hermaphrodite — meaning individuals begin life as females, and some may transition to male as they mature or in response to social dynamics. Males have been reported to maintain small harems of 4–5 females in the wild.
breedingNotes: Has been reported to have spawned in public aquarium settings but breeding in home aquariums has rarely if ever been documented. As a protogynous hermaphrodite, the dominant individual in a social group may transition from female to male. Courtship in the wild has been observed occurring around sunset with broadcast spawning of pelagic eggs. Generally considered not feasible for captive breeding at the hobbyist level.

Original designated secondary (Reef2Reef thread) returned HTTP 403; FishBase used as replacement secondary.

additional.name: Monaco Nature Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Confirmed protogynous hermaphrodite status, native range countries, harem-forming behavior, and adult size rarely exceeding 20 cm in practice (max recorded 26 cm).

fish.breedingNotes: The Flagfin Angelfish is a protogynous hermaphrodite and a broadcast spawner, releasing eggs and sperm freely into the water column. Spawning has been reported in public aquarium settings at dusk, but successful home aquarium breeding has not been documented.
